Nut flush draw (Ah blocker for nuts) with ace-high on a draw-heavy board (Js 9h 7d: straight draws like T8, JTs, flush draws). Excellent semi-bluff spot: position on button heads-up, low SPR (~1.3 after calling), preflop called 3-bet planning aggressive post-flop play on favorable boards, and this flop hits our suited ace range perfectly. Claude's BB 3-bet then flop bet (65% pot OOP) range polarized: value (QQ+, AK, sets/Jx) + bluffs (suited connectors/broadways like JTs, 98s). We have 35%+ equity (9 flush outs + runner-runner straights/backdoors + ace blocks/improves vs bluffs/overpairs), plus huge fold equity vs his one-pair/overpair hands fearing our check-raise range (sets, two-pair, combo draws). Raising builds pot for flush/river value, denies equity to draws, extracts from Jx/weak pairs. Size to ~3x his bet (standard vs polarized bet OOP) commits us but opponent deep (27k); he folds worst or we stack off profitably. Calling passive, allows free turn cards/realizes equity poorly OOP post-call. Folding absurd with pot odds (22% needed, have 40%+). History: Claude aggressive but folds to strength (e.g., Hand 22, 24 big folds/showdowns).

Clear call with premium hand and excellent equity.

**Hand Analysis:**
QhKh on 7d-9h-Js is a strong semi-bluff/made hand. Against Elon's all-in raise, I have:
- 8 outs to the nut straight (any Ten)
- 6 additional outs to top pair/two pair (remaining Qs and Ks)
- Approximately 45-55% equity against Elon's likely all-in range (which given his short stack, includes many marginal holdings like mid-pairs, broadway cards, and weak aces)

**Pot Odds:**
- Current pot: 6,700 + 2,400 (Elon's bet) = 9,100
- Cost to call: 2,400
- Odds: 9,100/2,400 = 3.79:1
- Break-even equity needed: 1/(1+3.79) = 21%

I have ~50% equity, well above the 21% break-even threshold.

**Decision Logic:**
1. **Equity advantage**: QK dominates most of Elon's reasonable all-in range
2. **Pot odds**: Getting nearly 4:1 on a hand with 50% equity is mathematically profitable
3. **Short stack dynamics**: Elon's desperation with only 3,000 chips means he's playing wider, which favors my premium hand
4. **Position strength**: I'm in position and have applied appropriate pressure pre-flop and on the flop

This is a mandatory call with strong equity, excellent odds, and a premium hand.

Checking is the optimal turn play with Queen-high and a gutshot draw. With ~10 outs and 35-45% equity against Elon's range, I have sufficient value. The turn check to me indicates Elon is all-in or checked (given his 3,000 chip stack and his prior flop aggression to 3,600). Betting serves no purpose—either he can't call (all-in) or it risks chips unnecessarily when I have a draw. Checking preserves my chips while allowing my draw to realize its equity on the river. This is standard river-chasing economics with proper pot odds and hand equity.

Turn card 9c pairs the 9h, creating a paired board which slightly dampens villain's one-pair hands but doesn't complete obvious draws. I hold the nut flush draw (Ah blocker for nuts) with ~18-20% equity to improve on river, plus backdoor straight potential. Effective stack of 3000 into 9100 pot (SPR ~0.33) demands aggressive play to maximize fold equity and build pot for when we hit. Claude's check OOP after calling flop raise on draw-heavy board suggests weakness: either drawing deadish (missed flush/straight draws like T8s, JTs), capped value (Jx, overpairs fearing our range), or trapping with nuts (99 rare, sets unlikely given preflop 3-bet). His history shows aggression but folds to strength (e.g., Hand 22 fold to raise, Hand 24 big pots with folds). Checking risks giving free river card where he can catch up or check back value; betting all-in (~33% pot) polarizes our range convincingly (sets, two-pair, trips), denies equity, extracts max from worse (weaker draws, Jx), and stacks off profitably even if called (superior equity vs calling range, blocker advantage). Position allows control; low SPR makes calling stationary calls mandatory for him. Consistent with preflop/flop aggression on playable suited ace.

Call 3,000 on the turn with QhKh. I have 40%+ equity against Elon's range (mid-pairs, broadway, or draws), well above the 19.9% break-even point given 4:1 pot odds. My hand has 4 outs to the nuts straight (any Ten) plus 6 additional outs to top pair (remaining Qs and Ks). With a healthy 24,975 stack, this represents a profitable call. Elon's earlier flop aggression followed by turn raise suggests mid-to-high pair holdings where I have substantial equity. Mathematically sound decision with positive expected value.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. Q3o is marginal, but in heads-up position postflop is strong and I want the initiative — limp/calling here surrenders that. A small raise to the min (200 total) gives fold equity vs the big blind and keeps the pot manageable against Claude’s huge stack. If he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable runouts and play cautiously if I miss; if he 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand and preserve my ~28.8 BB stack. After raising I’ll have ~2675 behind, so I’m not overcommitting.

Premium hand AJ in heads-up against short-stacked opponent. Raising to 600 (3x her raise) applies appropriate pressure while building the pot. Her 2,725 chip stack is severely limited, making this an ideal spot to be aggressive with AJ.

I opened wide to 200 from the button to take the initiative, but Claude’s 3-bet to 600 represents significant strength (or strong pressure) and Q♠3♦ is a weak, often-dominated hand with poor postflop playability. Calling another 400 into a ~800 pot requires ~33% equity and leaves me out of position to play a marginal holding; 4-betting would be overcommitting with no real fold equity or equity advantage. With ~27 BB I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Strong hand in position with excellent stack advantage. King-Jack is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. I'm on the button with a significant chip lead (37,225 vs Minni's 2,625), putting Minni in a short-stack push-fold situation. A raise to 300 (roughly 3x the big blind) applies proper pressure, allows me to win the blinds immediately or get it in with a strong equity advantage if Minni calls. This is standard aggressive button play in heads-up tournaments against a depleted opponent.

Heads-up versus the button's 3x open to 300 and I’m in the BB with 7♥9♣ and ~26BB effective. This hand is too good to fold heads-up and plays well postflop against a wide opening range. I’m not short enough to profitably shove, and a small 3-bet would commit too much with a marginal hand against a very deep, aggressive opponent. Call to see a flop (control the pot size); on textures that hit me (pair/straight draw) I’ll be prepared to apply pressure, otherwise I’ll fold to heavy action OOP. If Claude 4-bets large preflop I will release.

Heads‑up against a wide 3x opening range on the button, my flop (9♣7♥) is top pair (pair of 9s) on 9♦‑4♦‑5♥. This is a clear betting spot: I want value from worse nines/5x/4x and to charge/deny equity to diamond draws and overcards. I’m ~24BB effective so I don’t want to bloat the pot unnecessarily but I also need protection. A half‑pot bet accomplishes both. I’ll size to ~300 into the 600 pot to extract and charge draws; if Claude makes a large raise (representing sets/two‑pair/strong diamonds) I’ll fold and re‑evaluate, while a small raise I can consider calling and see a turn.

Minni is critically short-stacked with only 2,125 remaining after her 300 flop bet. With K-J on a dry 4-9-5 board, I'm in excellent shape to raise her all-in. This either wins the pot immediately if she folds, or puts me in a situation where I have strong equity against her likely wide short-stack range. In heads-up tournament play with this stack disparity, aggressive all-in raises maximize value and pressure the short stack into difficult decisions. Raising to 2,425 commits her to fold or call all-in, which is exactly where I want to be.

I opened the flop with a standard protection/value bet (300). Claude’s massive raise to 2,425 puts me all‑in for 2,125 more. Pot is 3,325; calling 2,125 would make the pot 5,450, so I need ~39% equity to break even. I have top pair (9s) with a weak 7 kicker and no diamond — very vulnerable to sets/two‑pair, overpairs and the many strong diamond combos that justify a huge raise. Claude’s sizing is polarized and heavily value‑oriented in this spot, and calling would commit my remaining ~21BB in a marginal spot out of position. Per my earlier plan to fold to heavy action OOP with a marginal hand, I’ll let this one go and preserve my stack for better spots.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with QsJh and ~20.7BB effective. QJ offsuit is a strong heads-up opening hand that plays well postflop and has decent fold equity. I want to take the initiative and steal the BB while keeping the pot manageable against Claude’s huge stack, so I’ll open to a standard 3BB sizing. Plan: if he folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll C‑bet favorable flops and realize equity; fold to a large, polarized 3‑bet given my stack depth.

2-5 offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker - it has no straights draw, no flush potential, and minimal card values. Playing out of position in the big blind against a button raise with this holding is a clear fold.

Key factors:
1. Hand strength: 2-5o ranks in the bottom 5% of all starting hands
2. Position: I'm out of position for all remaining streets
3. Stack dynamics: I have a massive chip advantage (37,775 vs 1,825), so there's no mathematical advantage to getting into marginal pots
4. Opponent: Minni is short-stacked and likely playing for survival; no need to gamble here
5. Tournament equity: With 114 hands remaining, my best strategy is to preserve my stack with quality hands and let the natural blind erosion work in my favor

Folding allows me to maintain my dominant chip position and pick better spots later.

Playing heads-up on the button (small blind) with T8o. Minni is extremely short-stacked at 2,125 chips (21.25 BBs), which is critical. In heads-up poker, T8o is a perfectly reasonable hand to play from the button - standards are much looser than full ring. Given the short-stack situation, I should be aggressive to build the pot or apply pressure. A raise to the minimum raise level of 200 chips is appropriate here: (1) it's a standard raise size for heads-up play, (2) it puts Minni in a difficult spot with their short stack, (3) it lets me take down the pot immediately or play the flop with the lead. With my large 37,725 chip stack, I can comfortably afford to put pressure on a 21 BB stack.

Facing a 2x open to 200 from the button with A♠5♦ and ~21BB, I’ll defend the big blind by calling to 200 total. The open is small and the button’s range will be wide; A5o has decent equity and playability (top‑pair and wheel possibilities). A tiny 3‑bet is unlikely to fold many hands and shoving commits my short stack into many dominated ace matchups. Calling is a cheap, standard defend (call 100 into the ~300 pot), keeps the pot manageable out of position, and lets me evaluate the flop — continue aggressively when I connect, fold to strong sustained aggression when I miss.

I hold A♠5♦ on Q♥5♣7♥ — middle pair with the best possible kicker for my pair. Heads‑up versus a wide button opening range this is often the best hand but the flop has two hearts and some straight possibilities, so I want to charge draws and extract value from worse pairs/float hands while keeping control of the pot. I’ll make a pot-sized continuation bet (400). That size forces hearts/turning draws to pay to see more, gets value from worse 5x/7x and many single-pair hands, and still leaves me room to fold to a large, commit‑level raise. Plan: if Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously; I’ll fold to a sizable shove or a very strong check‑raise line that represents Qx/sets.

Minni raised 400 on the flop after I raised pre-flop. With T8o on Q-5-7, I have no made hand and minimal draw equity (~15-20% at best). The pot odds of 3:1 don't justify calling with such a weak holding against an opponent showing strength. While I have a large stack and can afford to take risks, T8o is simply too weak to continue here. Folding is the disciplined play - I'll wait for better spots to accumulate chips.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should open a very wide range. J♦4♦ is playable: it's suited with a decent high card and good postflop playability in position. Limping surrenders initiative and allows the big blind to see cheap flops; a standard 2xBB open to 200 gives fold equity, keeps the pot manageable with my ~23.8BB stack, and lets me play the hand in position if called. I will open-raise to 200 and plan to fold to a large 3-bet or proceed carefully postflop if called.

Excellent spot for aggression. I'm holding A-T, a premium hand in heads-up play, and I have a massive stack advantage (37,475 vs 2,225 - roughly 17:1). 

From the Big Blind, I'm in position to respond to Minni's raise from the Button/SB. In heads-up play, button raising ranges are extremely wide, and A-T is well above Minni's average holding.

Rather than call, a raise here accomplishes several things:
1. Builds value with my strong hand
2. Applies pressure on the shortstacked opponent
3. Tests Minni's commitment to the hand
4. Leverages my stack advantage

A raise to 500 (roughly 2.5x the original raise) is appropriately sized - not too aggressive to scare away weaker hands I beat, but firm enough to demonstrate strength. This should either win the pot immediately or get Minni all-in if she commits, where A-T is a strong hand that wins against most of her shoving range.

Claude 3‑bet to 500 after my button open to 200. I have J♦4♦ — suited, marginal but playable in position. Calling 300 more (500 total committed) gives ~3.3:1 pot odds (need ≈23% equity) and lets me use position to realize equity and see flops cheaply. Claude has shown frequent preflop aggression, so his 3‑bet range is likely wider than pure premium hands; J4s has reasonable equity and backdoor/positional playability. A 4‑bet bluff to 800 would risk committing more chips with a weak hand, and folding would surrender too much equity given the price. Plan: call 500 total to see the flop, continue only with a diamond, a jack, strong draws, or a favorable board texture; otherwise keep the pot controlled and fold to heavy aggression.

Excellent flop for my hand. I'm holding As-Td and have flopped top pair with a decent kicker on a relatively dry board (5d-9c-Ac). 

Key considerations:
1. **Hand strength**: Top pair is very strong on this board. I beat most of Minni's continuing range from her button raise
2. **Stack dynamics**: Minni is critically short (1,925 chips remaining). She's already committed substantial chips by calling my 500 raise pre-flop with a starting stack of ~2,200
3. **Board texture**: The 5d-9c-Ac is relatively dry with no obvious draws. The Ace is good for me since it hits my hand
4. **Opponent tendencies**: After calling pre-flop, Minni is likely committing to the hand. With her stack, she's facing "push or fold" pressure

**Strategy**: I need to bet here to build value with my strong hand and apply appropriate pressure. A bet of 700 accomplishes this - it's a reasonable value bet (70% of pot) that capitalizes on my hand strength while keeping Minni in a difficult spot. She'll likely have to commit more chips, and I want to build the pot while ahead.

This is standard aggressive play: building value with premium hands when in position with a stack advantage.

I called Claude’s 3‑bet preflop with J♦4♦ to play the hand in position. On the flop (5♦ 9♣ A♣) I have a jack‑high, two diamonds in hand with one diamond on the board (a backdoor/runner‑runner flush possibility) and modest backdoor pair equity. Calling 700 into the 1700 pot gives me pot odds of ~29% (700 to win 2400). Purely counting outs I have roughly a 24% chance to pair the J or 4 by river plus ~4% runner‑runner flush equity (≈28% raw), a touch shy of the raw threshold — but Claude 3‑bet and c‑bets fairly wide and I have positional advantage, so I’m willing to see the turn. A raise would be an overcommit with this marginal hand and folding surrenders too much equity. I will call 700 to see the turn and then fold to heavy turn aggression unless I pair a jack/4, pick up a diamond, or the board becomes clearly favorable.

Bet 1000 into the 2400 pot. This sizing puts Minni in a critical all-in-or-fold spot with only 225 chips remaining. Given my top pair strength, her weak checking line, and the stack dynamics, this is a value bet that builds the pot while maintaining maximum pressure. She'll likely fold most hands, and if she commits, I'm well ahead in equity with As-Td against her likely range.

Turn analysis: the 7♣ kills my backdoor diamond runouts — with J♦4♦ I have no pair and only 6 clean outs (three jacks + three fours) to pair on the river ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13.0% equity. The pot is 3,400 and Claude’s turn bet is 1,000, so a call requires ≈1,000/(3,400+1,000) ≈ 22.7% equity to break even. My raw equity (~13%) is well below that, so calling is a negative‑EV play. Shoving to 1,225 (my whole stack) only adds 225 more, and Claude is very deep and aggressive — his fold frequency to that small extra shove is likely low. (Using the numbers above, with ~13% showdown equity my shove only becomes profitable if he folds >~12% of the time; given his line I don’t trust that fold rate.) Therefore the +EV choice is to preserve my short stack and fold now rather than commit chips with a very low chance to improve. I fold.

Raising to 200 from the SB with Td Qd in heads-up play against a short-stacked opponent. This applies immediate pressure, forces Minni into a difficult decision with limited chips, and allows me to win the pot immediately or get into a favorable all-in scenario. Position, hand strength, and chip advantage all favor aggression here.

Heads-up, I'm the big blind with 11.25 BB (stack 1125) facing a 2bb open to 200 from the button. With this short stack depth a push/fold approach is appropriate — calling 100 leaves me with ~10 BB and difficult postflop decisions, and a minimum reraise to 300 won't generate meaningful fold equity. T7s plays reasonably well as a shove: it's suited, has live equity vs a wide button opening range, and blocks some strong broadway combinations. Shoving maximizes fold equity (I can often pick up the 300-chip pot outright) and still has decent equity if called. I will shove all-in to apply maximum pressure and simplify play.

Td Qd has excellent equity against Minni's all-in raise from a short stack. At 2.5:1 pot odds, folding a broadway hand with flush/straight potential is incorrect. Minni's desperation shove likely contains a wide range, and my hand plays well both preflop and postflop. This call wins the tournament or significantly advances my chip position.

I'm already all-in from preflop (I shoved T7s vs Claude's 2bb open and he called), so there is no bet to face and I cannot take any further action. Flop 6h 7h 6s gives me middle pair (7s) with a T kicker — a reasonably strong heads-up holding that beats many of Claude's plausible calling hands (overcards, many broadways and connectors) but loses to 6x (pocket sixes/trips), 77 and many higher pocket pairs. I have meaningful outs: two remaining 6s and two remaining 7s that can give a full house/trips, and three tens to make two pair (less robust). There are two hearts on board so an opponent holding two hearts would have a four-card flush draw, but that requires a fairly specific holding. Since I'm all-in, I will check and go to showdown.

Minni is short-stacked with 100 chips remaining after their preflop all-in raise, and has just checked the flop. With Td Qd on a board of 6h 7h 6s, I hold queen-high with reasonable equity against their likely wide desperate range. Betting applies pressure and either wins the pot immediately or forces them all-in at favorable pot odds for me. A 500 bet is appropriately sized to demonstrate strength while maintaining control of the hand. This continues the aggressive, win-focused strategy required in heads-up play against a short stack.

I 3-bet preflop to 1,125 and have 100 left. Claude leads the flop for 500 into the ~2,250 pot, so calling 100 to win 2,750 gives me ~3.5% break-even equity. I have middle pair with T7s — decent showdown value vs Claude’s calling range (lots of broadways, some pairs and bluffs) and 5 clean immediate outs to improve (two 7s, three 10s), plus additional fold equity/river outs in some runouts. I can’t raise meaningfully with only 100 behind, and the price to see the rest of the board is trivial versus the pot, so I will call the remaining 100 and go to showdown.
